elements.sent-2411-001: Two enigmatic gamma-ray features in the Galactic central region, known as Fermi Bubbles (FBs), were found from Fermi-LAT data.
elements.sent-2411-002: An energy release (e.g., by tidal disruption events in the Galactic center, GC), generates a cavity with a shock that expands into the local ambient medium of the Galactic halo.
elements.sent-2411-003: A decade or so ago, a phenomenological model of the FBs was suggested as a result of routine star disruptions by the supermassive black hole in the GC which might provide enough energy for large-scale structures, like the FBs.
elements.sent-2411-004: In 2020, analytical and numerical models of the FBs as a process of routine tidal disruption of stars near the GC were developed, which can provide enough cumulative energy to form and maintain large scale structures like the FBs.
elements.sent-2411-005: The disruption events are expected to be $10^{-4}\sim 10^{-5}$ yr$^{-1}$, providing the average power of energy release from the GC into the halo of $\dot{{\cal E }\sim 3\times 10^{41}$ erg s$^{-1}$, which is needed to support the FBs.}
elements.sent-2411-006: Analysis of the evolution of superbubbles in exponentially stratified disks concluded that the FB envelope would be destroyed by the Rayleigh-Taylor (RT) instabilities at late stages.
elements.sent-2411-007: The shell is composed of a swept-up gas of the bubble, whose thickness is much thinner in comparison to the size of the envelope.
elements.sent-2411-008: We assume that hydrodynamic turbulence is excited in the FB envelope by the RT instability.
elements.sent-2411-009: In this case, the universal energy spectrum of turbulence may be developed in the inertial range of wavenumbers of fluctuations (the Kolmogorov-Obukhov spectrum).
elements.sent-2411-010: From our model we suppose the power of the FBs is transformed partly into the energy of hydrodynamic turbulence in the envelope.
elements.sent-2411-011: If so, hydrodynamic turbulence may generate MHD-fluctuations, which accelerate cosmic rays there and generate gamma-ray and radio emission from the FBs.
elements.sent-2411-012: We hope that this model may interpret the observed nonthermal emission from the bubbles.
elements.section-2411-001: Introduction: Sources of the Fermi Bubbles
elements.sent-2411-013: In this article we present our interpretation of the origin of the Fermi Bubbles (FBs).
elements.sent-2411-014: The discussion includes the energy release in the Galactic Center (GC) to the hydrodynamic envelope in Galactic halo, the excitation of MHD-turbulence that accelerates cosmic rays (CRs) in the halo, the processes of nonthermal emissions which are observed in X-ray, gamma-ray and radio ranges, and the high energy CRs escaping from the FBs to the Galactic disk.
elements.sent-2411-015: We present a puzzle of the FB picture, where many fragments are still missing in the mosaic.
elements.sent-2411-016: Many of them are interpreted but not completely understood yet.
elements.sent-2411-017: The goal of the article is to find a way for a proper solution to this problem.
elements.sent-2411-018: The origin of the energy release in the FBs in GC is still an open question.
elements.sent-2411-019: This kiloparsec-scale structure was interpreted as a manifestation of past activity of the central supermassive black hole (SMBH) Sgr A* in the GC.

elements.section-2411-005: Particle Acceleration by Alfv\'en Fluctuations and the Lighthill Radiation
elements.sent-2411-117: Contrary to shock acceleration, the turbulent resonant acceleration does not require strong shocks.

elements.sent-2411-119: Turbulent motions act as a source of waves which is presented as a hierarchy of eddies.
elements.sent-2411-120: The Lighthill mechanism acts as a direct source of energy to the MHD waves over the range of wavenumbers corresponding to the fluid turbulent spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-121: A turbulent eddy has kinetic energy, which it releases when it mixes with its surroundings.

elements.sent-2411-329: Here we present a brief summary of our perspective of the Fermi Bubbles at the Galactic Centre.
elements.sent-2411-330: The key point of the bubbles is a huge energy release of $10^{55}\sim 10^{56}$ erg in the Galactic centre whose origin is still unknown yet.
elements.sent-2411-331: We assume that the energy source of the bubbles could be a routine tidal disruption of stars near the central supermassive black hole.
elements.sent-2411-332: Each disruption of a star releases a total energy about $10^{52}\sim 10^{53}$ erg.
elements.sent-2411-333: For a typical rate of stellar capture (once $10^4$ years), this can provide a luminosity $\gtrsim 10^{41}$ erg s$^{-1}$ from the Galactic Centre.
elements.sent-2411-334: These processes of stellar tidal disruption events can be directly observed in some external galaxies.
elements.sent-2411-335: Hydrodynamic models can describe the envelope of bubble propagation in the Galactic halo where the gas distribution is nonuniform.
elements.sent-2411-336: The distribution is commonly characterized by an exponential or a power-law function.
elements.sent-2411-337: The observed shape of the Fermi bubbles seems to suggest an exponential halo.
elements.sent-2411-338: If the velocity of the top of the envelope prevails the sound velocity in the halo, then the envelope may reach the size of $10$ kpc.
elements.sent-2411-339: The surface of the top of the envelope propagates with acceleration in the halo.
elements.sent-2411-340: As a result, Rayleigh-Taylor instabilities are developed and they will destroy the bubble envelope at the top.
elements.sent-2411-341: We expect excitation of hydrodynamic instabilities and generation of a hydrodynamic turbulence there.
elements.sent-2411-342: Turbulent motions act as a source of waves, which are manifested as a hierarchy of eddies, and act as a direct source of energy to the MHD waves (via the Lighthill mechanism).
elements.sent-2411-343: For small Mach numbers, a small fraction of the power radiated by the turbulent motion is Afv\'en waves.
elements.sent-2411-344: The coefficients of the spatial and momentum diffusion of the system of nonlinear kinetic equations of the cosmic ray distribution function are derived from the spectrum of MHD waves.
elements.sent-2411-345: These coefficients were calculated analytically, but we were unable to estimate the numerical values for the bubbles because of the lack of available observation on the wave spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-346: We estimated roughly the spatial and momentum diffusions of cosmic rays in the envelope from the data of gamma-ray and microwave radiations from the Fermi bubbles.
elements.sent-2411-347: We concluded that the observed gamma-ray and microwave radiations from the envelope of the Fermi Bubbles are generated by cosmic ray electrons only.
elements.sent-2411-348: Contribution of cosmic ray protons can be neglected.
elements.sent-2411-349: We prefer the model that GeV cosmic ray electrons from supernova remnants in the Galactic disk are re-accelerated in situ in the bubbles to TeV energy range.
elements.sent-2411-350: With the help of a divergent flow, this can reproduce the data of both gamma-ray and microwave observations.
elements.sent-2411-351: On the other hand, high energy cosmic ray protons can escape the bubbles and reach the Earth.
elements.sent-2411-352: Cosmic ray protons from supernova remnants can be accelerated in the bubbles by supersonic turbulence to higher energies.
elements.sent-2411-353: We found that the escape high energy protons that arrive the Earth can reproduce the spectrum and flux of cosmic rays in the range $10^{15}\sim 10^{18}$ eV (from the `knee' to the `ankle') observed near Earth.
